file_name: AudibleInc_20001113_10-Q_EX-10.32_2599586_EX-10.32_Co-Branding Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Investment Distribution Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: "Spoken-Word Audio Products" means, collectively, Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products, Related Products and Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products.
perturbation.changed_text: "Spoken-Word Audio Products" means, collectively, Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products and Related Products.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products' from the definition of 'Spoken-Word Audio Products', the contract becomes internally inconsistent. Sections referencing 'Spoken-Word Audio Products' may now exclude 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products', leading to confusion about which products are covered. For example, Section 2.2(d) requires that substantially all 'Spoken-Word Audio Products' available through the Company Site are available through the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section. This requirement now becomes ambiguous concerning 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As of and following the Launch Date, Company will (a) maintain the Mirror Company Site, [***], (b) ensure that every page of the Mirror Company Site displays prominent, above-the-fold, graphical hypertext links (to be designed by ACSI and subject to Company's prior approval, which shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed) which, when clicked, return the user to the ACSI Site, together with such other branding and Trademarks of ACSI as ACSI and Company may agree upon to be appropriate (provided, that if the Parties cannot agree upon such branding and Trademarks, notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, ACSI shall have no obligation to establish or maintain the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section until such time as the Parties have agreed upon the same; and provided further, that any disagreement shall be subject to the escalation procedure specified in Section 2.8 [Escalation of Certain Disputes] below), (c) ensure that the Mirror Company Site does not contain links to any Web Site other than the ACSI Site (including, without limitation, to the Company Site), (d) ensure that substantially all Spoken-Word Audio Products (and any other products approved by ACSI) available through the Company Site are available through the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section (but subject to Section 9.2 with respect to Related Products), (e) [***], and (f) [***]. Subject to the foregoing, Company shall have the right to limit the number of promotional incentives (e.g. free give-aways, low-cost promotional offers and the like) that are offered through the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section based upon the volume of traffic if such volume were to place Company in commercially untenable position as solely determined by Company. Unless otherwise agreed by the Parties in writing, Company will be solely responsible for all pricing, delivery and fulfillment of any products, including Spoken-Word Audio Products offered by Company on or through the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section (including the Mirror Company Site).
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Spoken-Word Audio Products' as including 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products'. The changed text removes 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products' from this definition. The contradicted text, Section 2.2(d), requires substantially all 'Spoken-Word Audio Products' available through the Company Site to be available through the Spoken-Word Audio Sub-Section. This now creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the requirement in Section 2.2(d) also applies to 'Non-Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products' since they are no longer part of the definition of 'Spoken-Word Audio Products'. This creates ambiguity regarding which products are covered by the obligation in Section 2.2(d), creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: "ACSI Site" means that Web Site, the primary Home Page of which is identified by the URL www.amazon.com (and any successor or replacement Web -------------- Site). For the avoidance of doubt, the "ACSI Site" does not include any other Web Sites maintained by or for ACSI or its Affiliates (including, without limitation, those Web Sites, the primary Home Pages of which are identified by the URL's www.amazon.co.uk and www.amazon.de).
perturbation.changed_text: "ACSI Site" means any Web Site maintained by Amazon.com Commerce Services, Inc. (ACSI) and its Affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'ACSI Site' from specifically www.amazon.com to *any* website maintained by ACSI introduces ambiguity. The original definition clearly excluded international Amazon sites. Now, references to 'ACSI Site' become unclear. This contradiction impacts Section 2.5, where the restriction on using Referral Information to target users of the 'ACSI Site' becomes vague. Does it now include users of amazon.co.uk or amazon.de? The impact extends to Section 9.1.1, regarding Exclusive Spoken-Word Audio Products, clouding whether ACSI can offer these products on international sites.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Company will not disclose any Referral Information to any third party (except for third parties acting directly on behalf of Company in connection with Company's internal business and who have agreed in writing to maintain the same in confidence), or use or permit any third party to use any Referral Information to target communications specifically to users of the ACSI Site, primarily on the basis of such persons' being users of the ACSI Site, without ACSI's prior written consent (provided, however, that nothing in the foregoing shall prohibit Company from contacting its own customers generally (including contacting users of the ACSI Site as part of any such general contacts) or prohibit Company from using non-personal statistical information regarding such users in the aggregate for any purpose without ACSI's consent). [***]
perturbation.justification: The changed definition of 'ACSI Site' broadens its scope from a specific website (www.amazon.com) to *any* website maintained by ACSI and its Affiliates. This directly contradicts the original intent and creates a strong in-text contradiction with the Contradicted Text. The Contradicted Text restricts the use of Referral Information to target users of the 'ACSI Site'. Because the definition of 'ACSI Site' has changed to include more websites, the restriction becomes broader and potentially unenforceable, impacting the original intention of the agreement. This ambiguity creates uncertainty and a direct contradiction, making the use of Referral Information unclear and potentially leading to disputes.

perturbation.original_text: "Confidential Information" means, with respect to either Party, all information relating to such Party or its Affiliates that is designated as confidential or that, given the nature of the information or the circumstances surrounding its disclosure, reasonably should -3- be considered as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information" means, with respect to either Party, all information relating to such Party or its Affiliates that is specifically designated in writing as confidential and delivered with a cover sheet labeled CONFIDENTIAL. No other information is considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' included both designated and reasonably considered confidential information. By restricting it to only information *specifically designated in writing* and *delivered with a cover sheet labeled CONFIDENTIAL*, any unwritten confidential information is no longer protected. This creates a contradiction in Section 11.4, which requires each Party to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party 'at a minimum, will take precautions at least as great as those taken to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature'. The modified definition would not protect undocumented trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party will protect the Confidential Information of the other Party from misappropriation and unauthorized use or disclosure, and at a minimum, will take precautions at least as great as those taken to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event with less than reasonable care. Without limiting the foregoing, the Receiving Party will: (a) use such Confidential Information solely for the purposes for which it has been disclosed; and (b) disclose such Confidential -21- Information only to those of its and its Affiliates' employees, agents, and consultants who have a need to know the same for the purpose of performing this Agreement and who are informed of and agree to a duty of nondisclosure. The Receiving Party may also disclose Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the extent necessary to comply with applicable Law or legal process or pursuant to a registration report or exhibits thereto filed or to be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, listing agency or any stock securities commission, or any other associated filings, provided that the Receiving Party uses gives the Disclosing Party not less than ten (10) days' prior written notice thereof and complies with all reasonable requests of the Disclosing Party to minimize the extent or scope of any such disclosure. Upon request of the other Party, or in any event upon any termination or expiration of the Term, each Party shall return to the other or destroy (and certify in writing such destruction) all materials, in any medium, which contain, embody, reflect or
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only include information specifically designated in writing and delivered with a cover sheet. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Section 11.4), which requires each party to protect the other's 'Confidential Information' with precautions at least as great as those taken to protect its own. The original definition included information 'that, given the nature of the information or the circumstances surrounding its disclosure, reasonably should be considered as confidential,' a clause that is completely removed. Because undocumented but clearly confidential information is no longer protected by the new definition, Section 11.4 is contradicted because the 'reasonable care' a party takes to protect its own confidential information may not now extend to the other party's undocumented confidential information.
